title: How Does ChatGPT Work? A Plain-English Explanation
seoTitle: How Does ChatGPT Work? Plain-English Guide
metaDescription: How does ChatGPT work? A beginner-friendly walkthrough of the GPT model, pretraining, next-token prediction, RLHF assistant tuning, and the chat loop.
oneLiner: ChatGPT is a GPT language model, tuned to act like a helpful assistant, wrapped in a chat interface that predicts your reply one token at a time.

blocks.md: **ChatGPT** is a product: a powerful language model wearing a friendly chat interface. The model underneath is a **GPT** (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) - a giant pattern-matching engine that has read an enormous amount of text and learned to predict what word is likely to come next.

Here is the everyday analogy. Imagine the world's best autocomplete - the kind on your phone that suggests the next word, but trained on a library instead of your last few texts. Give it the start of a sentence and it guesses a sensible continuation. Now imagine that autocomplete was *also* coached, by thousands of human examples, to behave like a polite, helpful assistant rather than just finishing your sentence. That coached autocomplete, served through a chat box that remembers the conversation, is ChatGPT.

Three things make ChatGPT what it is: (1) a GPT **language model** trained to predict text, (2) an **assistant-tuning** layer that makes it follow instructions and feel helpful, and (3) the **chat loop** that turns your messages into context the model can read. This article traces all three end to end.

ChatGPT feels like magic, and that feeling hides a simple machine. Understanding *how* it works turns you from a passive user into someone who can steer it. Once you know it is predicting text - not looking up facts in a database - its strengths and its failures stop being surprising.

items: You will understand why it sometimes **confidently invents things** ([hallucination](/learn/llm-fundamentals/sampling-and-hallucination/why-llms-hallucinate)) - it is filling in plausible text, not retrieving truth.
You will know why it does not magically know today's news (its [knowledge cutoff](/learn/llm-fundamentals/sampling-and-hallucination/knowledge-cutoff-explained)).
You will write better prompts once you see that everything you type becomes the model's *context*.

You will recognize that ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are all instances of the same recipe - a [large language model](/learn/llm-fundamentals/llm-basics/what-is-an-llm) plus assistant tuning - so the skills transfer.

blocks.md: In short: knowing how ChatGPT works is the difference between being amazed by it and being effective with it.

ChatGPT is built in stages. A raw model is trained on text, then specialized into a helpful assistant, then deployed inside a chat loop that runs every time you press enter. The diagram below shows the full journey from raw internet text to the reply on your screen.

blocks.text: How does ChatGPT work, step by step?

diagram.title: From internet text to a chat reply

steps.label: Pretraining
steps.sub: read the internet, learn to predict the next token

steps.label: Instruction tuning
steps.sub: learn to follow requests, not just continue text

steps.label: RLHF
steps.sub: learn human preferences for helpful, safe answers

steps.label: Chat loop
steps.sub: your messages + system prompt become context

steps.label: Your reply
steps.sub: generated one token at a time

blocks.md: First, OpenAI trains a [transformer](/learn/llm-fundamentals/transformers-and-attention/what-is-a-transformer) neural network on a vast amount of text - books, websites, code, and more. The training task is dead simple: hide the next word and make the model guess it. Repeat that trillions of times and the model is forced to absorb grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, and writing styles, all just to get better at guessing. This stage is **pretraining**, and it is where the model's raw knowledge comes from. (A deeper treatment lives in [how LLMs work](/learn/llm-fundamentals/llm-basics/how-llms-work).)

The model never deals with whole words directly. It breaks text into [tokens](/learn/llm-fundamentals/tokens-and-tokenization/what-is-a-token) - chunks roughly the size of a syllable or short word - and works on those. At each step it looks at all the tokens so far and outputs a probability for every possible *next* token. It picks one, appends it, and repeats. This loop is called [next-token prediction](/learn/llm-fundamentals/text-generation/next-token-prediction), and it is the only thing the model fundamentally does - even a paragraph of fluent reasoning is just this loop running over and over.

blocks.text: Step 3: Turning a raw model into an assistant

blocks.md: A freshly pretrained model is a brilliant text-continuer but a terrible assistant. Ask it a question and it might reply with *more questions*, because that is a plausible continuation of internet text. Two extra training stages fix this. **Instruction tuning** shows the model thousands of example request-and-good-response pairs, teaching it that a question should be *answered*. Then **RLHF** ([reinforcement learning from human feedback](/learn/fine-tuning/preference-training/what-is-rlhf)) has humans rank competing answers; those rankings train a reward model that nudges the assistant toward replies people actually prefer - helpful, clear, and safer. RLHF is the layer that makes ChatGPT *feel* like it is trying to help you.

sections.title: The chat loop and the system prompt

blocks.md: Here is the part most people miss: **ChatGPT has no memory between requests.** The model is stateless. Every time you hit enter, the app rebuilds the entire conversation into one long block of text - a hidden **system prompt** (instructions like *you are a helpful assistant*), then every previous message, then your new one - and feeds the whole thing in as [context](/learn/llm-fundamentals/context-windows/what-is-a-context-window). The model reads all of it and predicts the assistant's next reply, token by token.

diagram.title: What the model actually reads on each turn

layers.label: System prompt
layers.sub: hidden rules: 'You are ChatGPT, a helpful assistant...'

layers.label: Model predicts the next reply
layers.sub: generated one token at a time

blocks.md: This explains a lot. The 'memory' of a long chat is really just the transcript being resent each turn - which is why very long conversations can hit the [context window](/learn/llm-fundamentals/context-windows/exceed-context-window) limit and older messages start getting dropped. It also explains why [prompt engineering](/learn/prompt-engineering/prompting-basics/what-is-prompt-engineering) works: you are literally editing the context the model reads before it predicts.

blocks.title: A simplified view of what gets sent on one turn
blocks.code:
[system]  You are ChatGPT. Be helpful, accurate, and concise.
[user]    What's the capital of France?
[assistant] Paris.
[user]    What's the population?      <- your new message

# The model now predicts the [assistant] reply that comes next,
# one token at a time, using everything above as context.

blocks.md: Because the model only sees the current context, it cannot 'remember' a fact from a chat last week unless that fact is re-supplied. Built-in memory features work by *re-injecting* saved notes into the context - they do not change the model's weights.

The 'GPT' under the hood gets upgraded often, so treat any specific version as a snapshot. As of mid-2026, ChatGPT's default model is from the **GPT-5.5** family, which [OpenAI released in April 2026](https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-5/); the fast *GPT-5.5 Instant* variant became the default for free users in May 2026. Paid tiers add heavier 'thinking' and 'pro' variants for harder problems. The exact name will keep changing - what stays constant is the recipe: a GPT language model plus assistant tuning.

rows: The default; chatting, drafting, simple questions
The hardest tasks, often paid-tier only

blocks.md: Model names date fast. Rather than memorizing versions, remember the pattern: a *fast* model handles most chats, and a *reasoning* model is available when a problem needs more careful thinking.

sections.title: Key limitations to keep in mind

blocks.md: Once you see ChatGPT as a next-token predictor, its weaknesses become predictable rather than mysterious.

items: **Hallucination.** It generates *plausible* text, not *verified* text, so it can state false things with total confidence. Always check important facts. See [why LLMs hallucinate](/learn/llm-fundamentals/sampling-and-hallucination/why-llms-hallucinate).

**Knowledge cutoff.** Its built-in knowledge stops at the date its training data ends; without a live tool it cannot know recent events. See [knowledge cutoff explained](/learn/llm-fundamentals/sampling-and-hallucination/knowledge-cutoff-explained).
**No real-time memory.** It only knows what is in the current context window, not your past chats unless they are re-supplied.

**Math and counting slips.** Because it predicts tokens rather than calculating, it can miscount letters or fumble arithmetic - the famous [strawberry problem](/learn/llm-fundamentals/tokens-and-tokenization/llm-strawberry-problem).
**Confidently wrong tone.** RLHF makes it sound helpful and sure of itself, which can mask mistakes.

blocks.text: Sampling: why the same prompt gives different answers

blocks.md: The model outputs a probability for every next token, but it does not always pick the single most likely one. A setting called [temperature](/learn/llm-fundamentals/sampling-and-hallucination/llm-temperature-explained) controls how much randomness is allowed: higher temperature makes ChatGPT pick less-likely tokens more often, producing more varied (but sometimes wackier) replies. That is why asking the same question twice can give different wordings.

Modern ChatGPT is more than the bare model. It can call tools - search the web, run code, read files - and stitch the results back into its context before answering. Retrieving facts and pasting them into context is the same idea behind [RAG](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag), and it is the main way the product works around the knowledge cutoff and reduces hallucination on factual questions.

blocks.text: The reward model: alignment in one sentence

blocks.md: RLHF works by training a separate *reward model* to imitate human preferences, then using reinforcement learning to push the assistant toward answers that score high on that reward. This is the technical heart of *alignment* - getting the model's behavior to match what people want rather than just what is statistically likely. The foundational write-up is OpenAI's InstructGPT paper, which introduced the three-stage pipeline of pretraining, then instruction tuning, then RLHF that ChatGPT still follows.

blocks.text: ChatGPT is one product among many

blocks.md: Everything here generalizes. Claude, Gemini, and open models like Llama are built from the same blueprint: pretrain a transformer, instruction-tune it, apply preference training, wrap it in a chat or API loop. ChatGPT is simply the most famous instance. If you understand it, you understand the category - which is exactly why this is the first thing to learn about [how LLMs work](/learn/llm-fundamentals/llm-basics/how-llms-work).

faq.q: How does ChatGPT work in simple terms?
faq.a: ChatGPT is a GPT language model that predicts text one token at a time. It was trained on huge amounts of text (pretraining), then tuned with human feedback (instruction tuning and RLHF) to behave like a helpful assistant. The chat interface feeds your messages plus a hidden system prompt into the model as context, and the model predicts the assistant's reply.

faq.q: What model powers ChatGPT right now?
faq.a: As of mid-2026, ChatGPT's default is the GPT-5.5 family, released by OpenAI in April 2026, with a fast 'Instant' variant as the free-tier default and heavier reasoning and pro variants on paid tiers. The exact version changes often, but the recipe - a GPT model plus assistant tuning - stays the same.

faq.q: Does ChatGPT actually understand what I say?
faq.a: Not in the human sense. It detects and continues patterns extremely well, predicting likely next tokens given your message. That can look like understanding, but there is no consciousness or grounded knowledge underneath - which is why it can be fluent and wrong at the same time.

faq.q: Why does ChatGPT make things up?
faq.a: Because it generates plausible text rather than retrieving verified facts. When it lacks the right information, it fills the gap with something that statistically fits, producing confident but false answers. This is called hallucination, and it is why you should verify anything important.

faq.q: Does ChatGPT remember my previous conversations?
faq.a: By default the model is stateless - it only sees the current conversation's context. Long chats can exceed the context window and lose older messages. Memory features work by re-injecting saved notes into the context each turn, not by permanently changing the model.

faq.q: What is RLHF and why does it make ChatGPT feel helpful?
faq.a: RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) trains a reward model on human rankings of answers, then nudges the assistant toward replies people prefer. It is the step that turns a raw text-continuer into something that follows instructions and sounds helpful and polite.

furtherReading.title: InstructGPT: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (OpenAI, arXiv)
